seo.title: Ubon Ratchathani Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Explore Ubon Ratchathani Province: Pha Taem National Park, Sam Phan Bok, the Candle Festival, Khong Chiam two-color river, and the Mekong border. Area: 15,626 km², population: 1.87 million. Plan your visit.

provinceNameThai: อุบลราชธานี
overview: Ubon Ratchathani is Thailand's largest Isan province and one of the most historically and naturally significant in the northeast. Sitting at the confluence of the Mun and Chi Rivers on the Khorat Plateau, it shares borders with Laos and Cambodia, giving it a uniquely multicultural heritage shaped by Lao, Khmer, and Thai traditions. The province is famous for its spectacular Ubon Candle Festival (Khao Phansa), the prehistoric cliff paintings of Pha Taem National Park, the otherworldly rock formations of Sam Phan Bok on the Mekong, and the famous two-color river at Khong Chiam where the Mun meets the Mekong. The glowing temple Wat Sirindhorn Wararam (the 'Pantone temple') has become a modern pilgrimage site. Ubon is known as the 'City of Beautiful Lotuses' — a fitting nickname for a place that blends deep spirituality with stunning natural scenery.
geography: Ubon Ratchathani covers 15,626 km², making it one of Thailand's largest provinces. The terrain is dominated by the Khorat Plateau, dissected by the Mun River flowing eastward to meet the Mekong at Khong Chiam. The province's eastern edge forms a natural border with Laos and Cambodia along the Mekong River. Pha Taem National Park in the northeast contains dramatic cliffs overlooking the Mekong, prehistoric rock paintings estimated at 3,000 years old, and the bizarre sandstone formations of Sam Phan Bok — often called the 'Grand Canyon of Thailand.' The landscape transitions from dry deciduous forests in the interior to lush riverine habitats along the Mekong.
highlights: Ubon Candle Festival (Khao Phansa) — Thailand's most spectacular wax sculpture festival, held every July at the start of Buddhist Lent with elaborate parade floats
Pha Taem National Park — cliffside prehistoric rock paintings 3,000+ years old overlooking the Mekong River, plus the Sam Phan Bok rock garden
Sam Phan Bok — thousands of potholes and rock formations carved by the Mekong into a surreal 'Grand Canyon of Thailand' landscape (accessible Nov–May)
Khong Chiam — Thailand's easternmost town where the brown Mun River meets the blue Mekong, creating a visible two-color river phenomenon
Wat Sirindhorn Wararam (the 'Pantone temple') — a modern glowing temple decorated with thousands of LED-lit Buddha images, dramatically illuminated at night
Pha Mung — 'Thai Grand Canyon' cliff viewpoint on the Mekong offering some of the most dramatic river panoramas in all of Isan
Ubon Ratchathani National Museum — showcasing Dvaravati, Khmer, and Lan Xang artifacts tracing the province's rich cultural crossroads history
Thung Si Mueang park — the spiritual heart of the city, where the famous giant wax candle sculptures are displayed during the July Candle Festival

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool and dry season — the best time for outdoor exploration. Temperatures 20–32°C with low humidity. Perfect for visiting Pha Taem cliffs, Mekong riverbank, and Sam Phan Bok rock formations which are only accessible when Mekong levels are low.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Jul (Candle Festival): Despite being monsoon season, the Ubon Candle Festival in July is a bucket-list event. Hotels book out weeks in advance. The rest of July–October features lush greenery but the Mekong rock formations at Sam Phan Bok are submerged.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Mar–Jun: Hot season with temperatures reaching 38–42°C — among the hottest in Thailand. Air-conditioned sites like the National Museum are more comfortable. Sam Phan Bok remains accessible until the rains return in June.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the best all-round weather. If the Candle Festival is your goal, book accommodation in Ubon city at least 4–6 weeks in advance for the July Khao Phansa weekend. For Sam Phan Bok, plan a visit between November and April when the Mekong is low.

topAttractions.name: Pha Taem National Park
topAttractions.description: A 340 km² national park on the Mekong cliff edge containing Thailand's largest prehistoric rock art site — cliff paintings of fish, humans, and geometric patterns estimated to be 3,000–4,000 years old. The park also features Sam Phan Bok (thousands of Mekong-carved rock pools), Pha Mung cliff viewpoint, and stunning sunrises over Laos. Best visited October to May when access roads and viewpoints are clear.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 300 THB foreigners / 100 THB Thai nationals (~$8 USD). Children under 14 free

topAttractions.name: Sam Phan Bok
topAttractions.description: Known as the 'Grand Canyon of Thailand' or the 'Mekong Rock Garden,' Sam Phan Bok is a 3 km stretch of the Mekong riverbed where thousands of potholes — some up to 10 meters deep — have been carved into the sandstone over millennia. Only accessible November to May when the Mekong recedes. Located within Pha Taem National Park near Khong Chiam.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Included with Pha Taem National Park entrance (300 THB foreigners / 100 THB Thai)

topAttractions.name: Khong Chiam — Two-Color River
topAttractions.description: A small town at the very eastern tip of Ubon Ratchathani where the reddish-brown Mun River flows into the blue-green Mekong, creating a striking two-color (mae nam song si) effect visible from the riverside viewpoint. The town itself is peaceful and dotted with guesthouses, seafood restaurants, and a riverside market. One of Thailand's most photographed natural phenomena.

topAttractions.name: Wat Sirindhorn Wararam (Glowing Temple)
topAttractions.description: A spectacular modern Buddhist temple in Sirindhorn District, about 60 km southeast of Ubon city. The main hall is decorated with an extraordinary mosaic of thousands of small Buddha images inlaid with glowing colored glass, creating a shimmering tapestry of color. At dusk and night, the illuminated exterior is mesmerizing — it has become one of the most photographed temples in northeast Thailand.

topAttractions.name: Ubon Candle Festival (Khao Phansa)
topAttractions.description: Held every July on the first day of Buddhist Lent (Khao Phansa), this is Ubon Ratchathani's signature event and one of Thailand's most spectacular regional festivals. Enormous wax candle sculptures — some up to 10 meters tall — are carved by local artisans and paraded through the city on elaborately decorated floats. The sculptures are displayed in Thung Si Mueang park throughout the Lent period.

topAttractions.name: Ubon Ratchathani National Museum
topAttractions.description: Housed in a former palace of the provincial governor, the museum showcases one of the finest collections of Dvaravati, Khmer, and Lan Xang artifacts in Isan. Highlights include prehistoric bronzeware, Khmer stone lintels, Ban Chiang-era pottery, and ceremonial objects from the region's Lao-influenced Buddhist kingdoms. Essential context for understanding the cultural depth of Ubon and the wider Mekong region.

localFood: Larb Moo — spicy minced pork salad with toasted rice powder, mint, and lime; the definitive Isan dish eaten with sticky rice
Som Tam Isan — Isan-style green papaya salad pounded with fermented fish (pla ra), chili, and lime — sharper and more pungent than the Bangkok version
Khao Niao — glutinous sticky rice, the Isan staple eaten with every meal, torn off and dipped into sauces by hand
Moo Ping — grilled pork skewers marinated in oyster sauce, garlic, and coconut milk; sold at every morning market in Ubon city
Gaeng Om — a rustic northeastern herbal stew made with pork or chicken, dill, lemongrass, and roasted rice powder; unique to the Isan region
Kai Yang — Isan-style grilled chicken marinated overnight in lemongrass, galangal, and fish sauce; best eaten fresh off the charcoal at roadside stalls
Nam Tok Moo — warm salad of grilled pork slices in toasted rice powder and herb dressing, a staple of Ubon market lunches
Pla Mekong Pad Cha — stir-fried Mekong giant catfish with chili, kaffir lime leaf, and galangal; Khong Chiam riverside restaurants serve this fresh from the river
culture: Ubon Ratchathani is a cultural crossroads where Thai, Lao, and Khmer traditions have blended over centuries of trade and migration along the Mekong and Mun rivers. The province's majority population traces Lao ancestry and the local dialect (Isan) is closely related to Lao. Buddhism is deeply embedded in daily life — Ubon has over 400 temples and the Buddhist Lent festival (Khao Phansa) in July transforms the city with its extraordinary wax candle sculpting tradition, recognized nationally as a symbol of Isan artisanal heritage. The nearby Mekong border communities maintain strong ties with Laos, and the town of Khong Chiam hosts a cross-border market. The ancient Khmer heritage is visible in temples and stone lintels scattered across the province.
gettingThere: Ubon Ratchathani Airport (UBP) has daily direct flights from Bangkok (Suvarnabhumi and Don Mueang) with Thai AirAsia, Thai Smile, and Nok Air — flight time approximately 1 hour 20 minutes, fares from 800–2,500 THB. By train, overnight sleeper trains depart Bangkok Hua Lamphong or Bang Sue Grand Station daily, arriving at Warin Chamrap station (across the river from Ubon city) in approximately 11–12 hours; second-class sleeper fares start from 500 THB. By bus, VIP overnight coaches depart Bangkok Mo Chit terminal and take 8–9 hours, with fares from 500–800 THB. From neighboring Isan cities, local buses and songthaews connect Ubon to Sisaket (1.5 hrs), Amnat Charoen (1.5 hrs), and Yasothon (2 hrs).
gettingAround: Within Ubon Ratchathani city, songthaews (shared pickup trucks) are the main local transport at 10–20 THB per ride. Tuk-tuks and motorcycle taxis are widely available for short trips (40–100 THB). To reach provincial attractions like Pha Taem, Sam Phan Bok, and Khong Chiam, renting a motorbike (150–250 THB/day) or hiring a songthaew driver for the day (500–800 THB) is the most practical option — public transport to these sites is limited. Grab ride-hailing operates in the city center. For Wat Sirindhorn Wararam (60 km southeast), a hired vehicle or tour is recommended as there is no direct public transport.
travelTips: The Candle Festival parade takes place on the first day of Khao Phansa (typically early-to-mid July) — book hotels 4–6 weeks in advance as the entire province fills up
Sam Phan Bok rock formations are only accessible November to April when the Mekong is low enough — they are completely submerged during monsoon season
Rent a motorbike in Ubon city for day trips to Khong Chiam (58 km) and Pha Taem — the riverside road via Route 2222 is scenic and well-paved
Warin Chamrap is across the river from Ubon city — the train station is there, not in Ubon proper; a bridge connects them and tuk-tuks/songthaews cross regularly
Visit Wat Sirindhorn Wararam at dusk or after dark when the LED illumination makes the glowing temple truly spectacular
The National Museum is closed Mondays, Tuesdays, and public holidays — plan accordingly
Bring cash — ATMs are plentiful in Ubon city but scarce near Pha Taem and Khong Chiam
The border crossing to Laos via Chong Mek (south of Phibun Mangsahan) is open to most nationalities with Laos visa-on-arrival available at the crossing
